entry.title: Issue in Direct GRN automatic PO Net price
entry.content:
Hello Consultants, 

Recently, we have encountered an issue regarding direct GRN's automatic Purchase order fetches the previous PO's net price instead of the updated price for the random materials (not all materials). 

So, it's a sequential updating process. First, we update the info record using BAPI 'ME_INFORECORD_MAINTAIN_MULTI', and we include it in the wrapper function module (RFC). Second, we are posting GRN (101) using BAPI 'BAPI_GOODSMVT_CREATE'.  

The whole process runs in the OData api and it has been integrated with an external third-party application.  We post the goods movement (using above mentioned BAPIs) in SAP once we receive the payload data from the external system. 

These two BAPIs are executed sequentially per post call triggered from the external system. Hence, we used a wrapper function module for info record update, and cross check the info record and condition price related database tables (EINE, A067, and KONP) after execution of the info record update BAPI.  Now, we have two scenarios as follows,

i) Sometimes the database tables didn't update with the correct price for random materials, even though we used transaction commit BAPI, and wait upto 10 secs logic.

ii) Sometimes the database is updated with the correct price. However, the GRN BAPI auto PO picks the previous day's net price for random materials. 

Note: There are no custom enhancements at the backend, and the configurations have been checked along with MM consultants.

I am looking forward to your responses.

Many thanks in advance!

entry.title: Usage of Data Store and its Impact
entry.content:
We have a requirement to send latest data once a day from MDG to Receiver and we have few approach to fulfill the same. We are looking for best solution/suggestion.

We have a requirement to send Material master data (latest) from MDG via DRF to Receiver daily once and we have below 3 approach

1) MDG is already sending data from MDG using DRF in real time to CPI, CPI is sending to AEM and using other iflow, CPI is picking data from AEM and sending to receiver.
But as our requirement is to send data in batches once a day, so in this case, MDG sends data in batches once a day to CPI via DRF and CPI sends to AEM, from AEM, CPI again picks data and send to receiver.
As we dont have picking data from AEM (based on some schedule), so we have come up with approach.

2) As MDG already sending data to CPI in real time, so we can create another queue, pick from AEM and then send data to Datastore (overwrite the records based on key field) and then using another iflows, with timer/scheduler, CPI can pick all data from data store and send to receiver.
3) Use the same approach as option 2 and instead of using Data store, we can use other external database like datalake, etc.

Can you please confirm which one can be best suited here ? and any pros and cons of using DataStore (where we have to store data for 24 hours) or any other recommendation in a case where CPI has to pick data from AEM and send in batches to receiver ?

Regards,
Saurav Kumar

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026: SAP Community Speakers Series and How AI Simplified my Blogging
entry.content:
Hello everyone!

Today is a very exciting day, as SAP Sapphire 2026 Orlando is here, and we're announcing our 2026 SAP Sapphire speakers' series featuring: @PrakashNanduri  @PeterSpielvogel @NoorNaqvi97 @kpsauer @KaraReed @Daniel_Dukes @sudhakarsingh @Shaheen_Umer 

As you can see by looking at the SAP Sapphire catalog, AI and the innovations it brings are going to be a major focus. Before diving into the great blog posts by our community members, I want to talk about how AI innovations impact my daily work:

  • Upgraded Simplified Blogging methodology (AI-based)
  • How Simplified Blogging helps me combine my love for long walks and creating blog posts in no time
  • Personal branding in the era of AI and SAP Community contributions to it

Upgraded Simplified Blogging

Last May, I presented the concept of simplified blogging methodology. Currently, the methodology mainly targets event-related blog posts (by speakers and attendees), but later on it will be expanded to other topics as well. Today, I will highlight its foundations and recent improvements that enable you to quickly craft a high-quality blog post while maintaining your authentic tone and voice.

Simplified Blogging step by step

Before following these steps, please be sure to complete the SAP Community tutorial as it's required for blogging on the SAP Community and offers a lot of valuable tips..

1) Template/draft creation 

List and structure the topics you would like to cover in a clear template/draft. Select a distinct title for each paragraph/section (for this example I used a blog post about simplified methodology).

Here is a template for example based on the blog post you are reading now:

  • Introduction
  • Upgraded Simplified Blogging
  • Simplified Blogging step by step (you can create a preset title for each step)
  • Personal Branding in the Era of AI
  • SAP Sapphire 2026 speakers' series!
  • Call to action

You can remove the sections' titles that don't follow your flow and just keep the paragraph's text (like in the introduction and call to action sections in this post where they don't a have paragraph title).

2) Record yourself and generate a transcript 

Today, many applications offer the ability to record and create a transcript. Use the application that you usually use for meetings, but this time have the meeting with yourself :). Record yourself with the transcript option turned on, and before speaking about each topic, say the topic name and then talk about it. This will help the AI tool separate the different sections in the blog post based on your transcript. 

I love having these meetings where I record myself during my evening or morning walks. I believe those are the times when I'm most energized and creative. The work on this blog post was mainly done during a morning walk, as you can see in the picture below :). By they way in the background you can see one of the grapevines, among other amazing fruit trees, that make every walk tasty and naturally energized.

During my morning walk while practicing the upgraded Simplified Blogging Methodology and being happy about it:)

3) Transform the transcript into a template

Go to the AI tool you normally use and apply the following steps/prompts:

Write: "Please embed this transcript [paste transcript] into this template [paste template] while maintaining the original words and ensuring the grammar is correct." Then, you should get a filled template ready for review and improvement. There are better chances for this to work if, when recording, you said the topics out loud according to the template.

I also encourage you to experiment on your own and share in the comments section what worked best for you with your tool..

4) Review, validate and modify/improve as needed

Review the post, validate and ensure it reflects accurately what you wish to communicate.

5) Final grammar review

After you finish making your edits, you can ask the AI tool to give it another grammar and spelling check. This way you can ensure you're ready for publication.

6) Publish

Once you feel your post is ready and follows all relevant guidelines, publish it where you think it fits best as explained in section in the SAP Community tutorial. Please also ensure to follow our AI content guidelines  and the SAP Community ROE .

Personal Branding in the Era of AI

Another interesting aspect is how people are being perceived and recognized in the era of AI tools. A couple of weeks ago, I went into one of the AI tools and I asked "Who is Moshe Naveh from SAP?". I was astonished by the answer it gave me. It summarized my path since I joined 16 years ago. It covered my achievements, what I do and a lot more.

I then asked it, "How did you define this?" It replied, "I inferred it from publicly available SAP Community pages...". So, I recommend that everyone who wants to establish their online branding in the SAP world publish in the SAP Community. 

I continued the interesting conversation with the AI tool by asking other questions, such as why it chose to focus on the SAP Community. It also later gave me tips on how to improve my online visibility, and the topic for this post is part of that coaching.

I recommend that all of you enhance your online branding and become active on the SAP Community by:

1) Actively engage in discussions and questions 

2) Publish blog posts

3) Participate in different initiatives like Hello SAP Community, Hobbies series, events series and more 

Now, like with every great tool and capability, our SAP Community members can choose how to use AI in the way that best suits them and reflects their message. Some of our speakers preferred to write their posts in the old-fashioned way, while others used different AI-based methods, and they all created great blog posts! 

SAP Sapphire 2026 speakers' series!

Now, let's dive into the great blog posts written by our SAP Community members about their exciting SAP Sapphire sessions. These blog posts are a great way to get to know the SAP Community members who are going to speak at SAP Sapphire, learn about their topics, and engage with them through the interesting questions they ask the community to answer.

entry.title: Re: Unable to fill in Quantity in Purchase Order Price Un...
entry.content:
Good day @Iris59 

Thank you for your question.

You cannot fill the field Quantity in Purchase Order Price unit through the released Material Document API if it is not provided in the API metadata.

The supported approach for PO-based goods receipt, is for you to pass the goods receipt quantity using QuantityInEntryUnit and EntryUnit, together with the purchase order reference, purchase order item, movement type, plant, storage location, and batch where you deem it relevant. The quantity in PO price unit is then driven by the system based on the PO item, unit conversion, batch-specific UoM setup, and valuation logic.

Here is what I recommend:

  1. Do not add the field manually to the payload, because unreleased fields are not supported by the API contract.
  2. You should make use of QuantityInEntryUnit and EntryUnit in the goods receipt API payload.
  3. Check the purchase order item setup for order unit order unit, order price unit, price unit, and material UoM conversion. 
  4. Fix the original Batch-Specific Unit of Measure validation issue rather than using the PO price unit quantity as a workaround.
  5. If this field is required as an API input for your business process, consider raising an influence request. Influence Opportunity Homepage - Customer Influence

Here are references for more guidance

3352378 - Sample payload for API API_MATERIAL_DOCUMENT_SRV to post movement type 161 for blocked stock - SAP for Me

3027233 - sample payload for API API_MATERIAL_DOCUMENT_SRV to post movement type 262 - SAP for Me

362932 - Conversion with proportion/product units - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Your guide to SAP Signavio at SAP Sapphire, Madrid 2026!
entry.content:
Calling all process experts and transformation leads...

SAP Sapphire 2026 in Madrid is just next week so we’ve put together a guide for you on all must-knows related to SAP Signavio.  

We can´t wait to welcome you for the next part of our SAP Sapphire EMEA journey, where you will discover how SAP Signavio helps organizations turn potential into performance, from strategy to value through process excellence. This event series will also spotlight SAP´s bold new AI vision and reimagined Joule experience.

You´ll find us as part of the Business Transformation Management track again and we will be presenting our integrated toolchain which encompasses SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX and WalkMe, combined with Tricentis and Syniti. Over at SAP Signavio, we´ll be focusing on how you can support successful process and ERP transformation with the power of processes and AI excellence.

You‘ll find here an easy to navigate breakdown of the key SAP Signavio activities such as keynotes and sessions you don’t want to miss. You will also discover how to talk to our experts, what we´ll be doing at the Experience Center and how to join us virtually. Make sure you stay to the end to hear about some of the extra entertainment we have in store for you!

SAP:

As companies deploy hundreds of AI agents, the real differentiator is no longer automation alone but instead how intelligence is captured and turned into value. Discover how SAP Signavio solutions maximize AI agent impact with the new frontier of process excellence. Learn how to transform collective operational intelligence into sustained impact.

SAP Signavio:

  • May 20, 2.30-2.50pm - Why high performers focus on process excellence with SAP Signavio solutions with Josèphe Blondaut SAP and Elena Cheremukhina Takeda Pharmaceuticals, Hall 10 Theater 17

 

Talk to our experts 🗣️

Join us for 1 on 1 sessions in our expert center, where you can meet our experts who will offer practical guidance helping you with all your seemingly unsolvable problems and questions. Also join us at our demo stations to experience hands-on demos of our latest solutions.

 

 SAP Signavio Demo Booths:

  • Manage, govern, and get more value from your agentic enterprise with SAP – BTM1364

entry.title: SAP Meetup in Cyprus: Networking and insights
entry.content:
SAP Stammtisch Cyprus: Real Talk on AI and the Core Dilemma

The time has finally come to gather the SAP community in Cyprus once again. This isn't just another networking event. It is a dedicated space for experts to sit down, reconnect, and dive deep into the challenges we face every day in the ecosystem. Our goal is simple: mutual support and high-level professional exchange in a relaxed, welcoming atmosphere.

For this session, we are putting two of the most pressing topics in the industry on the table:

1. The AI Revolution: Hype vs. Reality

AI is everywhere, but what is the actual "vibe" on the ground for SAP professionals? We want to move past the marketing slides and discuss the raw impact of Artificial Intelligence on our roles.

  • Will AI replace SAP consultants and developers? We will debate whether we are looking at a tool for efficiency or a total shift in the workforce.

  • Tangible Changes: How is the daily work changing right now, and what should we prepare for in the coming months?

2. SAP Standard vs. Custom Development: The Great Balance

The "Clean Core" strategy is the talk of the town, but real-world B2B requirements often tell a different story. We will share insights on how to navigate the tension between staying in the standard and building custom solutions.

  • When is Custom worth it? We’ll discuss the specific scenarios where a custom development provides a genuine competitive advantage and when it is just unnecessary technical debt.

  • The Decision Framework: What are the best practices for deciding between a standard process and a custom build? How do we justify these decisions to stakeholders?

Whether you are navigating an S/4HANA migration, managing a legacy ECC system, or looking to integrate the latest BTP innovations, your perspective is valuable. Join us for an evening of pragmatic discussion, shared expertise, and great company.

Let’s cut through the noise and get to the heart of SAP.

entry.title: New User Interface - Performance Management | Category for Goals not displayed
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I am looking for clarification regarding goal categories and evaluation types in SAP SuccessFactors Performance & Goals Management (PMGM).

In a Performance Form, I have two types of goals:

  1. Goals from the Goal Plan

    1. Created via Manage Team Targets
    2. Example: Revenue (EURm)
    3. In the goal details panel, the category and evaluation type (e.g. linear target evaluation) are clearly displayed.

 

2. Manually created goal on the Performance Form

  • Created directly in the Performance Template (Add Goal → Create from scratch)
  • While editing the goal on the form, there is a Category dropdown and I can select the same value (e.g. linear target evaluation).
  • However, after saving:
    • The category / evaluation type is not displayed in the goal details panel.
    • No target logic (baseline, thresholds, evaluation curve) appears.

 

If I can select a category (e.g. “linear target evaluation”) when creating a manual goal on the Performance Form, why:

  • is this category not shown later in the goal details?
  • and why does it not behave the same way as a Team Target created via Manage Team Targets?

Many thanks in advance for your help.

Best Regards,
Josef

entry.title: Re: We want the system not to automatically firm the plan...
entry.content:
Good day @Chinmay5 

Thank you for your question.

You cannot stop the planned order from being automatically firmed during partial conversion in Public Cloud. That is the standard behaviour. When a planned order is partially converted into a production order, the system creates the production order for the partial quantity and keeps the remaining planned order as fixed. The remaining planned order is only deleted once it is fully converted.

Here is what I recommend:

  1. If the remaining planned order must not become firmed, avoid partial conversion.
  2. Where possible, make sure you split the planning quantity into separate planned orders before conversion.
  3. Ensure you fully convert only the planned order quantity that should become a production order.
  4. If partial conversion must be used, manually review or unfirm the remaining planned order afterward if your process allows it.
  5. If your business requires automatic suppression of firming during partial conversion, consider raising a customer influence request. Influence Opportunity Homepage - Customer Influence

Here are references for more guidance

Creating Production Orders by Planned Order Conversion

3418163 - After you convert a planned order in Manage Material Coverage app, the production order generated is not released automatically. - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: RFHUDSP - non-deductable VAT
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

As far as I understand, there is a new requirement in Hungary according to which input VAT is no longer 100% deductible, but only 50% or 70%, depending on the case. From a technical perspective, this can be handled via tax codes by using condition type MWVS with account key VST, combined with MWVZ / NVV, and splitting the percentage accordingly.
In report RFHUDSP, this split is handled correctly, and the non-deductible portion of the input VAT is properly distributed to costs.

In addition, there is also a specific customizing table for report RFHUDSP: IDHU_VATPROP. In this table, it is possible to maintain the non-deductible percentage per tax code. In that scenario, 100% of the tax is posted to the input VAT account via the tax code, while the report splits the amount based on the maintained percentage. The non-deductible part of the input VAT would then have to be reposted manually.

Do any of you have experience with this topic and can share how you implemented it?
Do you see any advantage in using this reporting customizing instead of handling the split directly via tax codes? I am wondering why this additional customizing exists if the requirement can already be fulfilled using standard tax code functionality.

entry.title: Re: Clarification on PO Reference Handling in JournalEntr...
entry.content:
Good day @elmntx 

Thank you for your question.

For your requirement, the Public Cloud does not provide a standard workaround to pass the Purchase Order reference EBELN/EBELP in the Journal Entry Post API payload when you create a supplier down payment request.

The API can create a supplier down payment or down payment request using the supported accounting fields and DownPaymentTerms, but it does not provide the same purchase order reference linkage as the standard purchase down payment process. If the PO reference fields are not available in the API payload, you cannot manually add them as custom payload fields.

Here is what i suggest as a next step:

  1. Make use of Create Purchase Order Advanced app to maintain down payment data on the PO item.  
  2. Use Monitor Purchase Order Down Payments to create the supplier down payment request with PO reference.  
  3. Make use of Manage Supplier Down Payment Request for manual processing where you need to.  
  4. If the PO fields are missing in the supplier down payment request app, check the filed status setup as explained in KBA 2866313.
  5. Use Journal Entry Post API only where a direct accounting down payment request without PO linkage is acceptable.
  6. Consider raising a customer influence if API based PO linked supplier down payment request creation is mandatory for your business. Influence Opportunity Homepage - Customer Influence

See the following reference for more details.

3479539 - API for Manage Supplier / Customer Down Payment Requests App - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events
entry.content:
Welcome to week 2! Last week you published Business Partner events to a broker. This week, we shift to the **consumer side** — we will connect to that broker from an integration platform/runtime and consume those events.

This is where the "integration" in event-driven integration starts to take shape. Rather than a point-to-point call from producer to consumer, we're decoupling them via the broker. Your consumer doesn't need to know anything about where the events came from — it just subscribes to the topic and processes what arrives.
 

Your task this week

👉 Connect to your broker from an integration platform or runtime and consume the Business Partner events you published last week.

When an event is received, your integration should:

  1. Parse the CloudEvent payload
  2. Extract the data field
  3. Log or store the received payload (a simple log output or a file write is fine for now — we'll do something more interesting with it next week)

We haven't mentioned anything about storing the events or what would hapeen if the consumer is not available. Given how important business events normally are, you should consider using a storing mechanism, e.g. a queue, in case the consumer is unavailable and you don't want to miss any events.

Integration platform / runtime options:

  • SAP options: Cloud Integration (SAP Integration Suite), SAP Build Process Automation
  • Open-source / general options: Apache Camel, Node.js with your broker's SDK, Python with your broker's SDK, n8n

Your consumer does not need to be on the same technology stack as your publisher. Part of the beauty of a message broker is that producers and consumers are completely decoupled — they can be written in different languages, deployed on different infrastructure, and replaced independently.

Share your work

Add a comment in this discussion with:

  1. A screenshot or log output showing your integration receiving and parsing an event
  2. A brief description of what integration platform/runtime you used and how you connected to the broker (protocol used, e.g. AMQP, MQTT, REST)
  3. Any challenges you ran into connecting the two sides

SAP solution note — I'm sharing in the comments below how I solved this using SAP Integration Suite + Advanced Event Mesh. Also, I'm aware that some of you actually already completed this exercise as part of week 1 and that's completely fine. You can relax this week  🍹🏝

Some food for thought:

  1. What protocol did your broker support for consuming messages? Did you have a choice?
  2. What would happen in your setup if the consumer was offline when events were published? Would it receive them when it came back online? (This depends if you are storing those events, e.g. configuring a queue in the broker)
  3. How would you handle a malformed CloudEvent that arrives on the topic?

entry.title: Managing Complex HR Landscapes as a SAP SuccessFactors Customer: How Max Success Plan Supports
entry.content:
Managing Complex HR Landscapes as a SAP SuccessFactors Customer: How Max Success Plan Supports Multi‑Entity Organizations

SAP SuccessFactors may sit at the centre of your HR strategy, but it rarely operates alone: payroll, time and attendance, identity, analytics platforms, regional systems, and non‑SAP tools all orbit around it. Add multiple legal entities, regulatory requirements, and local operating models, and “keeping things consistent” becomes less about control and more about constant negotiation.

For most large organizations, the real challenge is no longer how to implement SAP SuccessFactors. It is how to keep a complex HR landscape coherent as it continues to evolve. This is where many transformations quietly begin to struggle.

When Complexity Turns into Fragmentation

Complexity on its own is not the problem. Large organizations are complex by design, and some degree of variation is unavoidable. The real risk emerges when complexity starts to fragment: when systems, processes, and decisions drift away from each other over time without a shared guiding structure.

Fragmentation often doesn’t show up as a failure, instead, it appears gradually: one region introduces a different process because it has unique constraints. Another adjusts an integration to resolve a local issue. A third adopts a release feature later, or not at all, because timing didn’t align. Each decision is reasonable in isolation. Together, they make the landscape harder to understand, harder to govern, and harder to scale.

Over time, organizations start asking familiar questions - why does the same employee action behave differently in different countries? Which data definition should analytics rely on? Who actually owns this integration now? Can we safely roll out a new capability globally without surprising half the organization?

Hopefully, you’ll agree with us that these are not configuration questions; they are governance questions.

Why Multi‑Entity HR Landscapes Are So Hard to Manage Over Time

In a multi‑entity environment, decisions about HR systems are rarely made by one team. Global HR may define standards, local HR may adapt them, IT may own integrations, partners may drive implementation choices. And over the years, people rotate, priorities change, and the original rationale behind decisions often fades or evolves to the point that it becomes unrecognisable as the time goes by.

It’s obvious, that what get lost here isn’t documentation, it’s context. And when context disappears, governance becomes reactive: teams spend more time explaining why something works the way it does than deciding what should come next. Change moves slower, not because people are resistant, but because no one has confidence in how many dependencies are attached to any given decision.

This is the reality many mature SAP SuccessFactors customers are operating in when they begin to consider Max Success Plan.

How Max Success Plan Supports a Multi‑Entity Reality

Max Success Plan does not attempt to simplify complex organizations into something they are not. Instead, it introduces structure and continuity around how complexity is designed, governed, and evolved.

Max customers work with HCM Solution Architects, Coaches, and Experts‑on‑Demand who engage across the full transformation lifecycle - from early discovery through long‑term operations. Their role is not to centralize control or replace local expertise. It is to maintain a coherent point of view across entities, systems, and partners as the HR landscape grows and changes.

This means:

  • architectural decisions are evaluated with long‑term scalability in mind, not just short‑term feasibility
  • Integration patterns are reviewed holistically, rather than region by region
  • Data migration and volume considerations are addressed as part of a broader data strategy, instead of being treated as one‑off projects.
  • UX and analytics decisions are aligned so insights remain meaningful across entities.
  • Operational readiness is assessed with downstream impact in mind.

In practice, this creates a framework where variation is intentional and governed, not accidental. SAP and your organization working hand in hand to ensure your transformation is tracking along the way it should be.

Global Standards That Enable, Not Block, Local Needs

One of the biggest concerns we often hear from the multi‑entity organizations is they fear that stronger governance will slow teams down. In reality, the opposite is often true.

When architectural principles, design standards, and decision boundaries are clear, local teams can move faster because they don’t need to negotiate every choice from scratch (or worry about rework later). Max Success Plan supports this balance through structured checkpoints such as architecture points of view, design evaluations, feasibility checks, and phase reviews.

These checkpoints are not imposed as control mechanisms; they rather exist to surface dependencies early, align global intent with local requirements, and prevent divergence from becoming irreversible. Over time, this reduces friction between global and local teams rather than increasing it.

Why AI Makes Fragmentation Even More Expensive

As AI‑enabled capabilities begin to influence HR processes, the cost of fragmentation increases sharply: inconsistent data models undermine confidence in AI insights; architectural shortcuts limit which innovations can be scaled; extensions built without a long‑term strategy quickly become blockers instead of accelerators.

In multi‑entity environments, AI amplifies whatever structural weaknesses already exist. Max Success Plan helps organizations approach AI‑driven HR innovation as a governed capability. Through expert oversight, innovation exploration, and architectural safeguarding, customers can assess where AI makes sense globally, where local variation is appropriate, and how to scale responsibly without compromising trust or readiness.

For SAP Max Success Plan customers, managing a complex HR landscape does not mean constant intervention. It means clarity:

  • instead of discovering issues late in the process (especially during rollouts, releases, or audits) risks are identified earlier, when decisions are still malleable
  • instead of accumulating exceptions, the landscape evolves with intent
  • instead of reacting to inconsistencies, teams understand why differences exist and how they fit into the larger picture.

Over time, organizations experience fewer surprises, clearer ownership across entities, and an HR platform that continues to scale rather than slowly hardening into something difficult to change.

When Is This the Right Conversation to Have?

If your SAP SuccessFactors environment spans multiple legal entities, geographies, payroll systems, or SAP and non‑SAP technologies, the challenge you are facing is not operational maturity. It is how to keep the whole landscape coherent as it continues to evolve. At that point, Max Success Plan stops being a question of service level and becomes a discussion about operating model and transformation governance.

The right next step is a conversation with your Customer Success Manager or Account Executive to assess whether your organization’s complexity calls for transformation‑level safeguarding, before fragmentation becomes a constraint rather than a by‑product of growth.

entry.title: BI 2025 – CSV Data Export Ignores Character Set Selection (ANSI/Windows-1252), Output Always UTF-8
entry.content:
Hi Community,
after upgrading from SAP BusinessObjects 4.3 SP3 to BI 2025 Batch 5, we noticed that CSV exports always produce UTF-8 output, even when ANSI (Windows-1252) is explicitly selected in the CSV options. This applies to both direct exports from the WebI document and scheduled exports to the file system. In 4.3 SP3 the behavior was correct.
Interestingly, the character set setting is respected within the WebI document itself – the issue only occurs on export.
Our assumption is that the root cause is the mandatory switch to Java 17 in BI 2025. Since Java 17 defaults to UTF-8 regardless of the OS, any export path that does not explicitly pass a character set will silently ignore the user's selection and fall back to UTF-8. In BI 4.3 with Java 11, the OS default (Windows-1252) was still the fallback.
Has anyone else run into this? Any workaround or official SAP feedback would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Hands-on! SAP SuccessFactors Sandbox now available on the Learning Site
entry.content:
You can now explore Joule on the SAP SuccessFactors Sandbox on the SAP Learning Site. 

Accessible with a valid SAP Learning Hub subscription, this sandbox system helps to explore, test, experiment, build use cases, and get a first look at Joule in SAP SuccessFactors. Suitable for all roles and levels, it includes sample data you can use to explore the functionality, experiment with scenarios, and test your results. 

Joule is the AI copilot that utterly understands your business and revolutionizes how you interact with your SAP systems. It makes every touchpoint count and every task simpler. It offers conversational interactions between humans and computers to simplify access to information and automate business processes. 

Joule is packed with powerful features that will transform how you engage with the practice system. Now you can benefit from Joule to: 

  • Navigate directly to any functionality without clicking through multiple pages. 
  • Complete tasks faster — Joule brings the action to you, so you don't need to switch between pages. 
  • Retrieve information from existing documents instantly, without having to contact HR. 

Joule

Watch this video to see Joule in action: >> Interacting with Joule in SAP SuccessFactors << 

A few things to keep in mind before you dive in: 

  • Your sandbox access is valid for 28 days — plenty of time to explore and experiment at your own pace. 
  • The sandbox is updated twice a year, so you always get to work with the latest SAP SuccessFactors features. 
  • Occasionally, the sandbox may be briefly taken offline for maintenance to keep everything running smoothly for you. 

Ready to get started? 

We believe that learners thrive when they have the freedom to explore. That is why we encourage you to experiment with Joule in our hands-on practice sandbox to see how it best fits your learning needs. 

With a valid SAP Learning Hub subscription, access the SAP SuccessFactors sandbox system and start testing. 

Click the Joule icon at the top navigation and dive in: 

Joule Icon

Based on your assigned user role, try asking Joule questions in natural language, for example: 

  •  “Let me set up my home address.” 
  • “Have I received or given any feedback?” 
  • “Who is Daniel Cortez in my organization?” 

Take your learning further with the free “Exploring the SAP Business AI Portfolio"  learning journey on the SAP Learning site — a great next step to explore the full potential of Joule and SAP Business AI. 

Documentation on SAP Help Portal:  https://help.sap.com/docs/joule/capabilities-guide/joule-in-sap-successfactors-4de4e7fb29434079ae07dffc93b64639?locale=en-US 

 

Happy learning! 

SAP Learning Services Topic Page  
Ask questions about Training and follow  
Read other Training blog posts and follow

entry.title: Re: PRODUCTION/QC/SALES TRANSACTIONS DONE THROUGH BARCODE...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello Ashish,

Thank you very much for your question. As it is rather complex, I asked our internal chatbot for support.

Here's the reply. Can you please check whether this approach works for you?

PP / QM Process Understanding

  1. Machining and Loading: After machining, ensure that the finished goods (FG) are loaded into the trolley according to its capacity to prevent damage.

  2. Transfer to Quality Inspection: Move the loaded trolley to the Quality Inspection area safely.

  3. Quality Inspection: The Quality Inspector checks the products to ensure they meet specifications. Only accepted quantities are processed further, and a QR code is generated based on this quantity.

  4. QR Code Generation: The QR code should include details like Production Order Number, Machine Number, Product Name, and Quantity.  It is generated only when stock is available in the system. For more information, see Barcode Scanning in Inventory Management and Production Order.

  5. Storage and Dispatch: After QR code generation, the trolley is moved to the Finished Goods area for storage or dispatch.

SD Process Flow

  1. Sales Order Creation: Use the Sales Order app (VA01/Fiori App) to create a sales order.

  2. QR Code Scanning: Implement a custom Fiori app for QR code scanning to capture necessary details.

  3. Outbound Delivery Creation: Use the Outbound Delivery (A2X) API Convergent Billing - SAP Community to create an outbound delivery. For more information, see Outbound Delivery (A2X).

  4. Picking and PGI: Automate picking and post goods issue (PGI).

  5. Billing Document: Automatically create billing documents.

  6. E-Invoice and E-Way Bill: Electronic Invoicing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public … - SAP Community Use existing configuration nodes and BADI for automation.

  7. Digital Signature: Utilize the BADI provided by SAP for digital signatures.

Custom Fiori App and Business Objects

  • Develop a custom Fiori app or Z table to store QR code data, including fields like Material, Quantity, Plant, Storage Location, etc.
  • Use Business Objects or EWM handling units for delivery creation and PGI.

Automation and Integration

  • Set up background jobs for VL10A and billing document creation to automate delivery, PGI, and billing processes.
  • For E-way bill automation, use BADI_EDOCUMENT_IN_EWB and related methods.

Additional Considerations

  • Ensure that the system checks for stock availability before QR code generation to maintain data integrity.
  • Implement a custom CDS to store trip details and billing information.

This approach leverages SAP S/4HANA Cloud’s capabilities to streamline the PP/QM and SD processes, ensuring efficiency and compliance with business requirements.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Purchasing Processing Time Not Accepting More Than 99...
entry.content:
Good @sai52 

Thank you for your question.

Question: Is there any standard limitation for this field in S/4HANA Public Cloud?

Answer: Yes, there is. The value 120 days cannot be maintained directly in Purchasing Processing Time because the standard accepted limit for this field is 99 days.

 

Question: Is there a way to extend or override this limit via configuration or SSCUI?

Answer: There is no standard SSCUI or configuration option to extend this field beyond the delivered limit.

 

Question: Has anyone handled a similar requirement, and what would be the recommended approach?

Answer: The approach I recommend is not to place the full procurement lead time into Purchasing Processing Time. This field represents the internal buyer processing time required to convert a purchase requisition into a purchase order. For a total procurement lead time of 120 days, split the timing across the correct scheduling components:

  • Purchasing Processing Time for internal PR to PO processing.
  • Planned Delivery Time for supplier delivery lead time.
  • GR Processing Time for internal goods receipt or warehouse handling time.

 

As a next step, I suggest you do the following:

  1. Keep Purchasing Processing Time within the accepted system limit.
  2. Maintain the supplier lead time as Planned Delivery Time in the relevant product or source-specific procurement data.   
  3. Maintain GR Processing Time where you require internal receipt handling.
  4. Run MRP and validate the purchase requisition released date and delivery date.  
  5. If your business requires the field itself to accept more than 99 days, you may consider raising an influence request.

Here are references for more guidance

2690138 - Modify/Delete Purchasing Processing Time - SAP for Me

3023022 - Purchase Requisitions Release Date is not as expected - SAP for Me

2523417 - "Scheduling according to purchasing info record or agreement" indicator is simplified in S/4 HANA system - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: ABAP XFT - Upload PDF to File Node
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I asked our internal chatbot for support.

Here's the reply. Can you please check whether it works for you?

Attaching a PDF to an XFT Node in ABAP

To attach a PDF document to an XFT node of type File in ABAP, you can follow these steps:

  1. Generate the PDF: You have already generated the PDF from SmartForms and converted the OTF data to PDF format (XSTRING).

  2. Identify the XFT Node: Ensure you have the header or identification data of the target XFT node where the PDF will be attached.

  3. Use the Attachments API: While there isn’t a direct mention of an XFT-specific API in the provided context, you can use the general Attachments API in S/4HANA Cloud to attach documents Create a pdf attachment using the Attachments API … - SAP Community to business objects. This API allows you to attach files to various objects, and you might be able to adapt it for your XFT node.

  4. Steps to Attach Using the API:

    • Set Up the Request: Use an HTTP client like Postman to set up the request. You will need to configure headers such as x-csrf-token, slug (file name), Content-Type (e.g., application/pdf), BusinessObjectTypeName, and LinkedSAPObjectKey.
    • Upload the File: In the request body, select the binary option and upload your PDF file.
    • Execute the Request: First, fetch the CSRF token with a GET request, then use it in a POST request to attach the file.

  5. Check the Attachment: After successfully attaching the file, verify by accessing the object in the system to ensure the PDF is correctly attached.

For more detailed guidance, you might want to explore the Attachments API documentation for S/4HANA Cloud, which provides comprehensive instructions on how to handle attachments programmatically.

If you are working in a specific SAP environment like ECC or S/4HANA, ensure that the APIs or function modules you use are compatible with your system version.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA, Ontologien und digitale Souveränität – Erfahrungen mit offenen Wissensgraphen?
entry.content:
ich beschäftige mich aktuell mit der Frage, wie sich SAP‑Prozesse in mittelständischen Unternehmen (50–500 MA) durch Ontologien und Wissensgraphen optimieren lassen – insbesondere im Hinblick auf digitale Souveränität und Vendor‑Unabhängigkeit.

Ausgangspunkt ist eine Beobachtung aus mehreren Projekten: Viele Unternehmen haben SAP‑Workloads in die Cloud (AWS/Azure) verlagert, ohne die Prozesslogik vorher zu optimieren. Das führt häufig zu deutlich höheren Betriebskosten, ohne dass die zugrunde liegenden Prozessprobleme gelöst wurden.

Mit der zunehmenden Nutzung von KI‑gestützten Ansätzen (z. B. SAP Joule, SAP Graph/Knowledge Graph, Palantir Foundry) rückt jetzt die Frage in den Vordergrund, wie Prozesslogik modelliert und langfristig kontrolliert wird.

Das Beispiel Merck zeigt, dass Ontologie‑basierte Optimierung erhebliche Effizienzgewinne ermöglichen kann. Gleichzeitig entsteht bei proprietären Plattformen schnell eine starke Abhängigkeit.

Daher meine Fragen an die Community:

1. Welche Erfahrungen gibt es mit Ontologie‑basierten Ansätzen auf offenen W3C‑Standards (RDF, OWL, SPARQL) im SAP‑Umfeld? Insbesondere im Zusammenspiel mit S/4HANA, BTP, Integration Suite oder Data & Analytics.

2. Gibt es praxistaugliche Wege, Prozesslogik über offene Wissensgraphen abzubilden, ohne in proprietäre Plattformen zu geraten? Zielgruppe: mittelgroße SAP‑Landschaften.

3. Hat jemand Erfahrungen mit europäischen Anbietern wie eccenca oder ähnlichen Lösungen, die auf offenen Standards basieren?

Ich freue mich über technische Einblicke, Architektur‑Erfahrungen oder Hinweise auf Projekte, in denen offene Ontologie‑Ansätze erfolgreich eingesetzt wurden.

entry.title: Re: Custom Field is automatically uppercase
entry.content:
Dear @kienngo,

The behavior you're observing is driven by the underlying ABAP Dictionary definition of the custom field, not by the value help itself. When a custom field is created in the Custom Fields app with Semantic Type Code List based on CDS View the framework generates the field as CHAR with the domain attribute "Lower Case" not set. This means any value assigned to the field is automatically converted to uppercase at the moment of assignment regardless of how the entry is displayed in the value help dropdown. In your case YY1_PR_RealRequestor is CHAR 95 which is exactly why the selected key is uppercased after picking it from the dropdown

It is worth highlighting that the value help CDS view (e.g. YY1_CustomBusinessUserVH) only supplies the list of entries shown in the dropdown it can return data in any case and the dropdown will display it accordingly. The uppercase conversion happens at the moment the selected key is written into the target CHAR field, which is a property of the target field itself, not of the value help view. Therefore building a new or modified CDS view will not change this behavior

In Public Cloud this is a hard design constraint:

  • The Custom Fields app does not expose any option to toggle the "Lower Case" attribute on a generated CHAR field
  • The Semantic Type of an already published field cannot be changed retroactively

Practical options:

  1. Keep YY1_PR_RealRequestor as the technical key. In Public Edition business user IDs in the user master are themselves uppercase, so the uppercased key still resolves correctly against the user master. What you lose is the visual mixed-case formatting of the embedded display name
  2. Add a complementary custom field of Semantic Type Text, populated via custom logic at save (e.g. BAdI on the Purchasing Document Item). The CHAR field continues to hold the technical key (used for validation, search, integration) and the Text field carries the human-readable, case-preserving label for UI and reporting. This is the standard pattern when both a controlled value and a readable representation are required.
  3. Recreate the field with Semantic Type Text only realistic if the field is not yet productive and contains no data. Note that this removes the CDS-based value help, since Text fields do not support code lists. For PR Real Requestor this is generally not a viable option

More details below:

  • https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2653718
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/key-user-custom-fields-when-to-use-what-type-of-code-list/ba-p/13557853
  • https://me.sap.com/notes/0002792571

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Public Cloud: How to include Freight Charges...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question.

I asked our internal chatbot for support. Please check whether the response works for you:

To address your requirement in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud for handling the ‘Fuel to Carrier’ charge (ZB01) without creating an open item in Accounts Payable, you can consider the following approach:

Solution Approach

  1. Use of Statistical Condition Type: Planned Delivery Cost of Purchasing in S/4HANA Clo… - SAP Community

    • Although you mentioned that making the charge type statistical is not specific per charge type, you can explore configuring the charge type ZB01 as a statistical condition in the pricing procedure. This way, it will not affect the MIRO process directly.

  2. Custom G/L Account Posting: Transportation Management ™ - Posting of Allocated Freight Costs

    • Configure the system to post the ZB01 charge to a specific G/L account that represents fuel consumption. This can be done by setting up a custom account key in the pricing procedure that directs the posting to the desired G/L account instead of the supplier.

  3. Manual Adjustment in MIRO:

    • During the MIRO process, manually adjust the ZB01 line to ensure it does not create a liability. This requires careful handling to ensure the material’s MAP is not adversely affected

Considerations

  • Ensure that any changes made do not disrupt the standard accounting and inventory processes.
  • Test the configuration in a sandbox environment before applying it to the production system to avoid any unintended consequences.

This approach should help you manage the in-kind fuel charge without creating an incorrect liability in the supplier invoice process.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Creating Statutory Reports for Non-Supported Countries in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are currently working on implementing statutory and legal reporting requirements for a country that is not officially supported by SAP standard localization content in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Our objective is to:

  • Create country-specific statutory reports
  • Adapt report layouts to local legal formats
  • Configure electronic or manual declarations
  • Use available SAP tools such as:
    • Define Statutory Reports
    • Compliance / DRC applications
    • Custom CDS Views
    • SAP Document and Reporting Compliance

We would like to understand the recommended approach and best practices for Public Cloud environments.

Main questions:

  1. What is the standard approach to create statutory reports for non-supported countries in SAP Public Cloud?
  2. Which applications or SSCUIs should be used?
  3. Is it possible to fully customize report queries, mappings, and layouts?
  4. Can custom CDS Views be integrated directly into statutory reporting frameworks?
  5. What are the limitations in Public Cloud compared to Private Cloud / On-Premise?
  6. Has anyone implemented local tax or financial declarations for unsupported countries (example: Morocco)?
  7. Is SAP Document and Reporting Compliance extensible enough for fully custom legal reports?

Any guidance, architecture examples, implementation steps, or lessons learned would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Billing control in Third-Party with Shipping Notification (BD3_GB) – Inbound Delivery dependency
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am currently testing the scope item BD3_GB in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, and I have a question regarding billing control.

In this scenario, using item category DB1 (required to enable the billing plan), I have observed that the system allows creating the final billing document (F2) even if the inbound delivery (VL31N) and the goods receipt posting (movement type 101, including GR for account assignment) have not been completed.

From a business perspective, the customer requires that billing should not be allowed if the inbound delivery has not been created or completed.

My questions are the following:

  • Is there any standard configuration that allows blocking billing until the inbound delivery is completed in this scenario?
  • Or is this behavior standard even in processes with shipping notification, meaning that the only possible options would be a manual billing block or custom logic (e.g., via BAdI)?

For example, if we use another item category such as CB1 (without billing plan), when attempting to create the final invoice (F2) without having completed the delivery, the system raises an error and does not allow billing to proceed.

This is exactly the behavior we would like to achieve with item category DB1 when using a billing plan.

 

Thank you very much in advance for your support.

entry.title: SAP Translation Hub Rolls Out Glossary Editor for Intelligent Data Reuse
entry.content:
Managing terminology in SAP Translation Hub used to require working outside the product. To update a glossary entry, you had to export your data to CSV, make your changes in a spreadsheet, and re-import the file, a process that added steps and introduced the risk of formatting errors each time a term needed updating.

That changes today. SAP Translation Hub now includes a glossary editor directly within the Intelligent Data Reuse component, giving you a dedicated UI to manage your terminology without ever leaving the product.

What's new?
The glossary editor lets you work with your terminology data in place:

Search and filter: Quickly locate the entries you need across your glossary, even as it grows.
Edit entries: Update source terms, target terms, or definitions directly in the UI, no CSV required.
Delete entries: Remove outdated or incorrect terms without touching a file.

What does this mean for your workflow?

The previous approach of exporting, editing, and re-importing a CSV file added unnecessary steps every time a term needed updating, and with manual file handling comes the risk of formatting errors, accidental overwrites, or data loss on re-import. The new editor eliminates these failure points entirely.

How to get started
The glossary editor is available now in the Intelligent Data Reuse component of SAP Translation Hub on SAP BTP (regions EU10, EU11, and US10). To access it:

1. Open SAP Translation Hub and navigate to Intelligent Data Reuse
2. Select Manage Glossaries in the left pane
3. Open a glossary to start searching, editing, or deleting entries

 

Learn More
SAP Translation Hub Documentation
Intelligent Data Reuse, Working with Glossaries
Contact us: translationhub@sap.com

Have questions about the glossary editor or how to get the most out of term-aware translation? Comment below or reach out to us directly.

my understanding is that as of today, there is currently no processor available in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud. However, I do believe SAP is planning to provide a successor.

In comparison: all other process steps contain a link to a specific app, like here:

 

I would suggest to do the following:

  • Create an improvement request on Homepage - Customer Influence and demand the release of an app for Public Cloud similar to F1619 Reprocessing Rate of Incoming Payments (which is available in Private Cloud). 

 

I hope this helps.

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: SAP Logistics Management: How to Integrate with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
Modern supply chains demand flexibility, transparency, and seamless execution across warehouse and transportation processes. With SAP Logistics Management, SAP introduces a cloud-native solution designed to meet exactly these needs—covering logistics processes end to end while integrating tightly with your core ERP landscape.

But SAP Logistics Management doesn’t operate in isolation. Its true value is unlocked through native integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, enabling organizations to extend their logistics capabilities without disrupting existing ERP processes.

How the Integration Works

Integration with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition is a central design principle of SAP Logistics Management.

Beyond ERP integration, SAP Logistics Management also connects seamlessly with:

  • SAP Business Network for Logistics, enabling collaboration with carriers, warehouse operators, and logistics service providers
  • Third-party logistics providers, allowing companies to extend their logistics ecosystem beyond SAP-managed environments

This integrated approach ensures that logistics processes remain synchronized across internal systems and external partners, reducing manual effort and improving execution quality.

Learn It Live: Integration and Configuration in Practice

If you want to understand how to integrate SAP Logistics Management, don’t miss the upcoming live session on June 3rd.

In this session, SAP experts will walk you through:

  • How SAP Logistics Management is integrated with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • How to configure integration with SAP Business Network for Logistics and third-party providers

Register Now

You can find full details and register here:

👉 Live Session

If you're looking to modernize your logistics processes while staying fully integrated with your SAP digital core, this session is one you won’t want to miss.

entry.title: SAP GTS - Legal Changes Hot info - May Edition
entry.content:
This blog edition is intended to provide updates on "hot" topics in relation to legal changes affecting the SAP Global Trade Services products in the month of May.  For previous editions please see the Past Editions section at the end of the blog.

This is not a replacement for the official notes released or the the Regulatory Change Manager announcements but rather supplementary information.  These announcements are also available in KBA  3707712 - SAP GTS - Legal Changes Hot info!!

11.05.2026
UK NTCS 6: Introduction of NCTS Phase 6 for transit shipments in the United Kingdom.
The communication with the United Kingdom's customs authorities (HMRC) for the transit procedure  is to be switched to NCTS phase 6. The Regulatory Change Manager has now been updated with the delivery of  SAP Note(3745794) in relation to the changes.

  • The legal change is mandatory for users of the NCTS system.
  • The proposed effective date is on June 1, 2026.
  • A trader test environment is expected to be available from April 2026.
  • This information is based on the information available at the time of publication of this blog. 

DE EMCS 2.7 for administrative documents in Germany

Communication with the German customs authorities is to be switched from EMCS 2.5 to EMCS 2.7.
The Regulatory Change Manager has been updated with the solving SAP Note(3715370)

Additionally you can find the rollout material Germany_EMCS_2.7_Documentation_Version_1.0.pdf attached to SAP Note(3715370)
The Version_1.0.  of the roll out material contains information on the following:

 

Past editions of this blog:

SAP GTS - Legal Changes Hot info - April Edition -  (CH PASSAR NE015 V5, NL DMS: Digitaal Dossier)
SAP GTS - Legal Changes Hot info
